Okay let's think.

I think I'm hobbled by a few things.

First, each document is kind of standalone so I miss context from adjacent documents. I should try harder to keep everything in one place unless there's a good reason to split things up.

Second, there's my desire to avoid making too many artifacts that won't be used for research or could be deprecated when the library is updated. I should try to keep a few core artifacts and otherwise render documentation directly from the source code, which is more likely to be maintained and updated.

What's the most bare documentation that gets the work done?

If template notebooks reliably got an overview and walkthrough each parameter and key functions, and I included a "setup" page explaining how to use them, the documentation for analyses and key workflows would probably be sufficient. 

The model pages are a bit more complicated. 
But one wonders if they should be. 

Let's lock in some decisions.
I certainly won't use nbdev to bind source code and documentation.
I won't even use show_doc, since it doesn't like my docstring style.

What's left?
How thoroughly will I document template notebooks? 
Clear overview, walkthrough of key parameters and functions, and a rendered example.
Oh, and I'll be a bit more literate in my presentation of code cells and their outputs.

Will they stand in for documentation of the library itself?
No, I will still generate an API reference from docstrings, but I won't worry about making it look nice. I'll just make sure the docstrings are clear and comprehensive.

From there, I still need to document models and workflows.
Workflows themselves can be documented like template notebooks.
So what's left is model documentation.
I have the memory and context components documented, so what's left is just CMR itself. 
I have the math for those; I just also need to relate them to the code and make sure I explain how the different components fit together.

Then there's the development guide / architecture stuff.
Data format page is still necessary.

The nbdev docs are kind of bad.
What are some docs I like?

Psifr's guide is pretty good.
But incompatible with the template notebooks, which are more focused on workflows and analyses than on the library itself.

So I have to decide whether I will lean into that style of documentation or write documentation that I'm ok being kind of bad.

Maybe claude can still write good documentation if I keep guide focused. Hmmm...
